questions.text: A restaurant lists a ₹300 dish on Swiggy. Swiggy charges a 28% commission. How much commission does Swiggy deduct from this single order?
questions.explanation: Calculation: Commission = Selling Price × Commission Rate = ₹300 × 0.28 = ₹84. The restaurant receives ₹300 − ₹84 = ₹216 before any other deductions (payment processing, packaging, GST). This is the most fundamental calculation in the sub-module - you must be able to do it instantly. ₹28 = 28 rupees flat (not a percentage calculation). ₹56 = 28% of ₹200 (wrong base). ₹96 = 32% of ₹300 (wrong rate).
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: C - ₹84

Calculation: Commission = Selling Price × Commission Rate = ₹300 × 0.28 = ₹84. The restaurant receives ₹300 − ₹84 = ₹216 before any other deductions (payment processing, packaging, GST). This is the most fundamental calculation in the sub-module - you must be able to do it instantly. ₹28 = 28 rupees flat (not a percentage calculation). ₹56 = 28% of ₹200 (wrong base). ₹96 = 32% of ₹300 (wrong rate).

questions.text: What percentage of every in-app purchase does Apple's App Store charge as commission from developers?
questions.explanation: Explanation: Apple's standard App Store commission has been 30% since 2008 - the same as Google Play Store. This is called the "App Store Tax." 15% is the reduced rate introduced in 2020 for developers earning less than $1M/year (the "Small Business Program"). 45% is YouTube's commission on ad revenue (higher than Apple's). 20% is not a standard platform rate. Apple's 30% rate became the subject of Epic Games' antitrust lawsuit (2020) and EU regulatory action (2024).
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: C - 30%

Explanation: Apple's standard App Store commission has been 30% since 2008 - the same as Google Play Store. This is called the "App Store Tax." 15% is the reduced rate introduced in 2020 for developers earning less than $1M/year (the "Small Business Program"). 45% is YouTube's commission on ad revenue (higher than Apple's). 20% is not a standard platform rate. Apple's 30% rate became the subject of Epic Games' antitrust lawsuit (2020) and EU regulatory action (2024).

questions.text: An Uber driver completes a ₹500 ride. Uber takes 25% commission. The driver spends ₹90 on fuel. What is the driver's net earning from this ride?
questions.explanation:
Step-by-step calculation:
Step 1: Uber's commission = ₹500 × 25% = ₹125
Step 2: Driver receives = ₹500 − ₹125 = ₹375
Step 3: Subtract fuel cost = ₹375 − ₹90 = ₹285
The driver earns ₹285 net - just 57% of the ₹500 the rider paid. Uber earns ₹125 without owning a car, buying fuel, or taking any physical risk. This illustrates the platform's value extraction model: own the network, tax the labour.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: B - ₹285

Step-by-step calculation:
Step 1: Uber's commission = ₹500 × 25% = ₹125
Step 2: Driver receives = ₹500 − ₹125 = ₹375
Step 3: Subtract fuel cost = ₹375 − ₹90 = ₹285
The driver earns ₹285 net - just 57% of the ₹500 the rider paid. Uber earns ₹125 without owning a car, buying fuel, or taking any physical risk. This illustrates the platform's value extraction model: own the network, tax the labour.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describes WHY restaurant owners cannot easily leave Swiggy even after commission increases?

options.text: Restaurants have signed long-term legal contracts that prevent leaving
Swiggy owns the recipes and food content of partner restaurants
Network effects mean a large percentage of their customer base now exclusively orders via the platform - leaving would eliminate that revenue instantly
Government regulations in India prohibit restaurants from operating without a delivery platform license

questions.explanation: Explanation: This is the classic network effect lock-in. Swiggy built a large customer base first. Now customers default to Swiggy when ordering food. A restaurant that leaves loses access to those customers - potentially 40 - 60% of their orders - immediately. The commission increase is painful, but losing 50% of orders is even more painful. Swiggy does not own contracts (A), does not own recipes (B), and no such government regulation exists (D). The trap is entirely economic, not legal - which makes it more insidious because there's nothing to challenge in court.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: C

Explanation: This is the classic network effect lock-in. Swiggy built a large customer base first. Now customers default to Swiggy when ordering food. A restaurant that leaves loses access to those customers - potentially 40 - 60% of their orders - immediately. The commission increase is painful, but losing 50% of orders is even more painful. Swiggy does not own contracts (A), does not own recipes (B), and no such government regulation exists (D). The trap is entirely economic, not legal - which makes it more insidious because there's nothing to challenge in court.

questions.text: Why is UPI peer-to-peer transfer considered "free" for the end user, while it still has a real cost to someone?

options.text: NPCI (government-backed) subsidises the infrastructure cost, which is ultimately borne by taxpayers and banks' other revenues
UPI uses no infrastructure at all, so there is literally no cost to anyone
Banks voluntarily cover the entire cost as a social service with no expectation of benefit
The sender pays the fee, but it is automatically waived by the government

questions.explanation: Explanation: Nothing is truly free. UPI P2P transfers have real infrastructure costs (servers, security, fraud management). NPCI, which is owned by a consortium of banks and backed by RBI/Government of India, absorbs these costs. Banks subsidise UPI to get users hooked on digital payments (from which they earn on other products - loans, FDs, insurance). The ultimate cost is distributed across taxpayers and bank customers through other fees. Option B is factually wrong (there is infrastructure). Option C is incorrect (banks do expect returns - data, cross-sell). Option D describes a waiver mechanism that doesn't exist.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: A

Explanation: Nothing is truly free. UPI P2P transfers have real infrastructure costs (servers, security, fraud management). NPCI, which is owned by a consortium of banks and backed by RBI/Government of India, absorbs these costs. Banks subsidise UPI to get users hooked on digital payments (from which they earn on other products - loans, FDs, insurance). The ultimate cost is distributed across taxpayers and bank customers through other fees. Option B is factually wrong (there is infrastructure). Option C is incorrect (banks do expect returns - data, cross-sell). Option D describes a waiver mechanism that doesn't exist.

questions.text: An Indian musician releases a song. It gets 5 million streams on Spotify India at ₹0.007 per stream. After paying 10% to their distributor (DistroKid), how much does the artist actually receive?
questions.explanation:
Step-by-step:
Step 1: Total Spotify payout = 5,000,000 × ₹0.007 = ₹35,000
Step 2: Distributor fee (10%) = ₹35,000 × 0.10 = ₹3,500
Step 3: Artist receives = ₹35,000 − ₹3,500 = ₹31,500
Five million streams. The artist earns ₹31,500. For context: if even 315 of those fans had bought the album directly at ₹100, the artist would have earned more. This calculation makes the streaming economy's exploitation viscerally clear.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: B - ₹31,500

Step-by-step:
Step 1: Total Spotify payout = 5,000,000 × ₹0.007 = ₹35,000
Step 2: Distributor fee (10%) = ₹35,000 × 0.10 = ₹3,500
Step 3: Artist receives = ₹35,000 − ₹3,500 = ₹31,500
Five million streams. The artist earns ₹31,500. For context: if even 315 of those fans had bought the album directly at ₹100, the artist would have earned more. This calculation makes the streaming economy's exploitation viscerally clear.

questions.text: A blockchain-based marketplace charges a 0.1% gas fee per transaction instead of a 28% platform commission. For a ₹10,000 order, what is the exact rupee saving compared to Swiggy's 28% commission?
questions.explanation:
Step-by-step:
Swiggy commission = ₹10,000 × 28% = ₹2,800
Blockchain gas fee = ₹10,000 × 0.1% = ₹10
Saving = ₹2,800 − ₹10 = ₹2,790
The blockchain option costs ₹10 instead of ₹2,800 - a 99.6% reduction in middleman cost. At scale (1 crore orders/year at ₹500 average), this represents ₹1,390 crore saved annually by restaurants/sellers - returned to the actual producers of value instead of extracted by a platform.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: B - ₹2,790

Step-by-step:
Swiggy commission = ₹10,000 × 28% = ₹2,800
Blockchain gas fee = ₹10,000 × 0.1% = ₹10
Saving = ₹2,800 − ₹10 = ₹2,790
The blockchain option costs ₹10 instead of ₹2,800 - a 99.6% reduction in middleman cost. At scale (1 crore orders/year at ₹500 average), this represents ₹1,390 crore saved annually by restaurants/sellers - returned to the actual producers of value instead of extracted by a platform.

questions.text:
Arjun runs a coffee shop in Pune. He lists his Signature Latte for ₹250 on Swiggy. His production cost (milk, coffee, sugar, electricity) is ₹85 per cup.

Platform details: Swiggy commission: 30% | Payment processing fee: 2% (applied after commission deduction) | Mandatory packaging: ₹18 per order

Calculate: (a) Arjun's net profit per cup via Swiggy. (b) Arjun's net profit per cup if a customer walks in and pays cash. (c) The percentage difference in profit between the two scenarios. (d) If Arjun sells 200 cups/month via Swiggy and 100 cups/month direct, what is his total monthly profit? (e) How much MORE would he earn if ALL 300 cups were sold direct?
Statement: "Blockchain eliminates all costs from a transaction. A restaurant selling biryani via a blockchain marketplace would have zero fees of any kind."
questions.explanation: Model Justification (Full 3 Marks):Blockchain does not eliminate all transaction costs - it dramatically reduces the platform middleman cost (from 28 - 35% to 0.1% gas fee), but several other costs remain unchanged. Government taxes such as GST (5% on restaurant food delivery) apply regardless of whether the sale happens via Swiggy or a blockchain marketplace, because tax law applies to the economic activity, not the technology used to facilitate it. Physical production costs - ingredients, electricity, cooking - are obviously unchanged. Logistics and delivery costs (driver's fuel, time) also remain, because blockchain governs payments and trust, not physical movement of goods. What blockchain eliminates is specifically the platform's rent-seeking commission - the fee Swiggy charges for being the matchmaker between buyer and seller. A blockchain smart contract can perform this matchmaking function at cost of ₹0.10 - 1 in gas instead of ₹75 - 140 in commission. So the correct statement is: "Blockchain dramatically reduces platform middleman costs while government taxes and physical costs remain." This distinction matters enormously for building realistic financial models for Web3 businesses.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Verdict: FALSE

Model Justification (Full 3 Marks):

Blockchain does not eliminate all transaction costs - it dramatically reduces the platform middleman cost (from 28 - 35% to 0.1% gas fee), but several other costs remain unchanged. Government taxes such as GST (5% on restaurant food delivery) apply regardless of whether the sale happens via Swiggy or a blockchain marketplace, because tax law applies to the economic activity, not the technology used to facilitate it. Physical production costs - ingredients, electricity, cooking - are obviously unchanged. Logistics and delivery costs (driver's fuel, time) also remain, because blockchain governs payments and trust, not physical movement of goods. What blockchain eliminates is specifically the platform's rent-seeking commission - the fee Swiggy charges for being the matchmaker between buyer and seller. A blockchain smart contract can perform this matchmaking function at cost of ₹0.10 - 1 in gas instead of ₹75 - 140 in commission. So the correct statement is: "Blockchain dramatically reduces platform middleman costs while government taxes and physical costs remain." This distinction matters enormously for building realistic financial models for Web3 businesses.

1 Mark: Correctly states FALSE
1 Mark: Identifies what blockchain DOES reduce (platform commission → gas fee)
1 Mark: Identifies what remains (GST, production costs, logistics) with correct reasoning
